After recently attending a regional conference in southern Europe where banking innovation and fintech were key topics, my interest in the banking sector renewed after some time and I think it is time to follow up on this stock I covered exactly a year ago, so you can call this a 1 year anniversary article.

Since my October 2024 coverage when I recommended holding on to this stock, it has surged nearly +24%.

At the time, I was positive on the firm's future potential growth from its fee-driven business segments, but I also thought it was somewhat overvalued at the time. Apparently, the market was even more confident, as momentum data shows this stock outperforming the S&P500 index over the last year.

Based on my updated research today, and a somewhat updated methodology, interestingly I came up with the exact same rating again of hold. Today's article argues that a neutral view is the best course on UBS after considering multiple upside/downside factors impacting the top and bottom lines, balance sheet, and price expectations.

The firm's greatest strength, from my view, is top-notch credit ratings and being a closely-watched systemically critical global bank we can expect it to undergo strict scrutiny on capital requirements, which I think is a good thing.

Rating Score: Hold

Growth Drivers: Certain Segments Driving Upside, As Innovation/AI Also Play A Role

First, we should talk about some growth drivers both from the perspective of the overall sector but also company-specific factors.

The role of AI and innovation cannot be ignored when talking about banking today. UBS even reiterated they are "investing in our future infrastructure and AI," in their Q2 results presentation.

A bank continually innovating can be an upside, particularly if those innovations can make processes more cost-efficient while also improving the client experience, not sacrificing it.

When thinking about the sector, UBS is in a niche focused on 3 segments: wealth, asset management, and investment banking. For instance, on its official website it boasts of its "tailored wealth solutions for families and entrepreneurs to achieve individual financial goals."

I think, therefore, an impact to future growth will come from demand for personalized wealth advisory. A study last February by global consultancy McKinsey & Co pointed to expected continued demand in the US market especially:

Looking ahead, the number of advised relationships will continue to grow; we estimate it could reach 67 million to 71 million by 2034, a 28 to 34 percent increase from 53 million relationships in 2024.

UBS, although based in Switzerland, has penetrated the US market for a long time now, particularly in wealth advisory, and in Barron's list of top private wealth management teams in the US for 2025, UBS-affiliated advisors had 5 of the top 30 spots, but the list also makes it clear that there is stiff competition from peers like Morgan Stanley (MS) and Merrill.

From a public policy and regulatory standpoint, because of the sheer size and systemically-critical role played by UBS, Swiss authorities in its home country have been tightening rules, as reported in Reuters just a few weeks ago:

The government has proposed stricter measures for UBS to avoid a repeat of the Credit Suisse meltdown and to shield taxpayers from losses.

I think this could be seen as an upside factor since it could lessen some future downside risks such a bank could face and increase capital requirements.

In the asset management space, I am looking for new client monies coming to the firm, as this can drive future fees income. Unfortunately, Q2 results reported net outflows of $2B:

Net new money (2.0bn), driven by net outflows in Fixed Income (excluding money market) and Multi-asset & Solutions, partially offset by net inflows in ETFs.

At the investment banking unit, there was better news, as it saw +13% YoY revenue growth:

In particular, UBS mentioned this was driven by the Global Markets business which includes activities like execution services, derivatives, and financing, all of which saw momentum.

The Personal/Corporate Banking segment saw a YoY decline, but the Global Wealth Management segment saw a +6% YoY growth, driven in part by "net new loans $3.4B driven by EMEA and Americas," which I think is a future upside factor as new loans made this year can drive future interest income.

So, my impression on growth factors discussed above are that on the one hand it seems UBS is innovating and also achieving results in a few segments driving upside potential, while also having some weakness in others and facing stiff competition in the US from peers as macro demand for financial advice continues. In this case, I am inclined to be on the low bullish side in the category of growth.

Profitability: Margins Trailing Key Peers, But Post-Merger Cost Cutting Still On Track

Next, let's talk about the topic of profitability.

With the next earnings call in a few weeks on Oct. 29th, for now as a run up to that we can touch on some points from the Q2 results.

Following trends in margins gives me some framework to understand how efficient this firm may be in generating profits, so in this case we can see the net income margin is 13.3%, much lower than the sector average.

Compared to similar peers, which I picked among global banks with some similar businesses including Morgan Stanley (MS), Deutsche Bank (DB), Barclay's (BCS), and Goldman Sachs (GS), UBS actually trails all of them on net margin:

I believe such data lowers the overall rating score for UBS, as it appears less efficient at profit generation than some key banking peers, with Goldman taking the helm in this category.

To be fair, it is understandable that UBS also went through a major integration after its takeover of Credit Suisse a while back, and it is still an ongoing process, with France24 reporting back in February that "it had achieved 58 percent of its target of achieving $13 billion in cost savings by the end of next year as part of the merger."

Further, in the Q2 results media release, UBS, mentioned that it "delivered further USD $0.7B in exit rate gross cost saves bringing cumulative cost reductions to USD $9.1B, or 70% of the USD ~$13B in expected gross saves."

So, on the one hand it seems that UBS could use a boost to its profit margin and thereby help drive future profitability growth, but it also has achieved considerable savings in the wake of the merger so that should be mentioned. Further, the analyst consensus points to 18 - 30% YoY EPS growth in the next two years, another positive point.

Given the combination of these factors, I am inclined towards a hold rating in this category.

Balance Sheet & Credit Rating: Top Ratings From Agencies, Although Loan Loss Reserves Creeping Up

Moving along, next we will discuss some elements of balance sheet risk.

Notable to mention is that from the UBS website we can see some strong credit ratings from major agencies, of the parent company itself, UBS Group AG:

That should add extra confidence to investors concerned about credit risk, but also it is expected that a bank like UBS will be watched closely as it is listed as one of global systemically important banks in the Financial Stability Board's 2024 list. Earlier, I also mentioned how Swiss regulators are cracking down further with new rules that UBS will have to abide by as well.

When it comes to debt vs equity, UBS with a D/E of 4.48 is on the higher end of the peer group I created below, perhaps indicating a higher reliance on debt and some investors may consider this an added risk:

From an additional angle, let's consider a key metric in banks and that is the CET1 ratio. In its Q2 results release, UBS had the following optimistic news:

In the second quarter, we maintained a strong capital position with a CET1 capital ratio of 14.4% and a CET1 leverage ratio of 4.4%. Both are in excess of our guidance of ~14% and >4.0%, respectively, and provide a solid capital buffer to requirements during the integration, while allowing us to self-fund strategic investments and return capital to shareholders.

Further, in terms of the balance sheet we should consider asset risk exposure. The largest asset on the balance sheet seems to be loans this bank made to others, to the tune of +$667B worth of them. This also means the bank can be exposed to loan losses.

We can see, for instance, that balance sheet data on Seeking Alpha shows that the allowance for loan losses, which is like a reserve the bank sets aside for anticipated losses, has seen a rising trend from March 2024 through June 2025, painting a picture of slightly elevated loan loss risk potential:

So, given the strong credit ratings and CET1, but also the elevated D/E and the growing trend of loan loss reserves, I am more inclined to saty in the neutral camp in this category.

Cashflow & Dividend Trends: A Dividend Growth Recovery, And Mixed Cashflow Picture

So far, this stock has been in the neutral/hold range across the board, so let's see if the topic of cashflow and dividend trends swings the needle either way.

With a below-average dividend yield of just around 2.2%, and only paying out annually rather than quarterly, I don't think it makes the case for high-yield investors seeking regular, frequent income. So, then it is a question of whether it is a dividend growth stock, and in that realm it has improved.

Consider that it reversed its dividend incline from 2021 to now be on a growth trend again, with a 5 year dividend CAGR of nearly +20%.

This is how that looks visually:

In terms of a cashflow metric tied to the core business, the cashflow from operations, we can see from my chart below that it has seen a considerable downward trend into negative territory over the last 2 years:

However, the nature of a major bank like this is such that it can also easily raise cash by selling tradeable securities/investments, and also borrowing via debt, which it seems to do quite a bit of. For instance, cashflow data shows in the quarter ending March it raised around $35B in cash from financing.

Therefore, I think the trends above should be taken in the context of the industry it is in. A REIT, for instance, may be sitting on a few billion of illiquid assets like property that it cannot easily convert to cash, whereas a bank like this can sell off a portion of its liquid securities (such as fixed-income assets) each trading day and raise cash, even though sometimes it may have to sell those securities at a loss.

So, the above dividend and cashflow case presents at least a neutral/hold justification at this point.

Valuation & Share Price: A Neutral View on Future Upside vs Current Price/Valuation

Coming around the corner now to the finish line, let's consider a forward-looking valuation metric and also talk about future price targets.

This stock's fwd P/E (GAAP-based) is 18.10, much higher than the sector avg. of 11.57, indicating some overvaluation is there.

In addition to the elevated valuation, along with the bullish momentum vs the S&P500 which I mentioned earlier, we can see also in the price chart I made below that the stock is trading lately well above its 200-day simple moving average:

So, there is not a mismatch between valuation and price since both are elevated.

Considering the positive analyst consensus on EPS growth for FY25 and FY26, I can see what could motivate the bulls to go after this stock, but at the same time I also showed in this article how UBS trails key peers in profit margins. This bullishness suggests the market may be expecting considerable margin improvement going ahead, particularly after the Credit Suisse integration fully completes finally.

In terms of future price targets, the Wall St consensus is saying an avg target of $41.16, which is only a +5.5% upside potential.

My own target is $42.17 (based on fwd P/E x consensus EPS estimate for Dec 2025), and this would be an upside of around +8% vs Wednesday afternoon's trading.

Considering the elevated valuation and share price, but also the potential price upside forecast of less than 10%, and my mixed sentiment about the ability of UBS to catch up to key global banking peers on profit margins, I am in the hold/neutral camp in this category too.

Conclusion: Agree With Consensus Across The Board

In conclusion, my own research today led to the exact same across-the-board rating of hold today in the consensus (Wall St, SA analysts, quant system).

Today's article presented 5 different categories which each supported a lukewarm/middle-of-the-road approach to this stock, reaffirming my prior sentiment on UBS yet again.

Don't fret, however, as a hold rating is not a negative outlook, but more of a cautious optimism looking ahead.

To consider a risk to my neutral/cautiously optimistic view of this stock, that has not yet been mentioned in this article or rating score, I would say a challenge could come from central bank decisions impacting interest rates. A continuation of elevated interest rates could negatively impact bond values, and earlier I've shown that this bank holds a lot of securities. However, it could also impact demand for loans, and this bank has a huge loan book it seems.

On the contrary, an extended period of low interest rates in the next several years could boost underlying bond values in their portfolio, and the cost of borrowing, so because we don't know too far in advance what central banks will decide for now it is a potential future risk investors will have to anticipate not just at UBS but at similar firms engaged in lending as well as holding large amounts of fixed-income securities.

data.title: MindWalk Holdings: Repositioned TechBio Platform With Early LensAI Proof Points
data.description:
Thesis

MindWalk Holdings (NASDAQ:HYFT), formerly ImmunoPrecise Antibodies, has completed a strategic pivot from a service-oriented CRO toward an asset-light, software-led techbio platform.

MindWalk’s core technology HYFT and commercial LensAI suite aim to compress discovery timelines and improve decision quality before wet lab spend.

Early outputs, such as an AI-generated GLP-1 agonist program with a dual-action (longevity-linked) pathway, identification of a universal dengue epitope candidate, and in silico immunogenicity [ADA] risk screening illustrate LensAI’s practical range.

With last-reported record quarterly sales of C$7.6 million, which is +45% YoY, a stronger balance sheet following the Netherlands divestiture, and retirement of a Yorkville debenture, MindWalk appears to enter a next phase with clearer operating focus.

The stock has moved up about 190% over the past year. I believe that at a $75 million valuation, the company is still quite undervalued. This the first coverage of the company on Seeking Alpha.

MindWalk Holdings

MindWalk Holdings currently operates under a single identity focused on in silico discovery and data products rather than capital-intensive wet lab services currently has three subsidiaries: ImmunoPrecise Antibodies, BioStrand and Talem. The company has transitioned from lower-margin services to a platform that monetizes via SaaS/DaaS, collaborations, and asset out-licensing.

HYFT is its main technology, and LensAI the AI-based drug development/discovery suite based on that technology. There is initial evidence that LensAI can generate candidate-level insights in metabolic and infectious disease while reducing safety risk through early immunogenicity screening. I will explain some sector- or company-specific terminology upfront.

Terminology

The company’s current focus is on in-silico discovery of therapeutics. ‘In-silico’ means that the drug discovery is computational; after the divestiture of the Dutch division, the company no longer has an actual lab or ‘wet lab’. This divestiture also allowed the company to focus on what it is most known for. HYFT is the technology developed within Biostrand by Belgian entrepreneur Dirk Van Hyfte. A ‘HYFT’ is a pattern in biology that represents the structure of that part of biology and its function. LensAI is the commercially ready software suite [API] which incorporates the HYFT technology, has been launched in June 2024 received the 2024 Impact Award, with a commercial release in October 2024.

Epitope mapping is the process of mapping binding sites on proteins. Epitope binning refers to the process of grouping epitopes based on similar binding sites. Multi-omics is a concept used to express the connectivity and integrated analysis of multiple elements of the study of biology, such as genomics, proteomics, metabolomics or transcriptomics.

MindWalk

Introduction

MindWalk represents a shift from services to an integrated platform-driven bio-native AI company.

MindWalk’s focus is on offering AI tools for drug and therapy development that takes a from-the-ground-up approach and tries to integrate multi-omics to create a knowledge graph of biology.

The HYFT technology is the foundation for that approach. HYFT is trained on various information including scientific literature, patient records and lab data. The HYFT technology encompasses 660 million HYFTs, 20 million structural HYFTs and entails 25 billion biologic connections.

The goal of the HYFTs is to feed the AI learning model with something more than raw sequences of data, which should allow the model to improve epitope discovery, perform and improve immunogenicity screening, make accurate predictions about the safety and efficacy of future drugs, better predict drug-drug interaction, and improve the design process of future drugs.

LensAI integrates DNA-RNA sequence protein structure which is the focus of other learning models such as the well-known AlphaFold, and text.

Business model

The business model of MindWalk is diverse.

Pharmaceutical or other companies can access the LensAI platform by paying a subscription fee. Additional analyses and usage of LensAI may lead to higher costs.

MindWalk also co-develops or outlicenses internally-developed drugs, which may lead to upfront payments, milestone payments or royalties.

MindWalk counts 19 out 20 big pharma companies among its total of over 750 active customers.

Compared to the traditional and currently divested business, LensAI drives higher margins of 90%.

One of the recent announcements in respect of the company’s core-business related to a partnership for the AI-based development of novel cancer therapeutics with a global biotechnology leader. The partnership is valued at $8 million initially, with the potential to expand to $10 million.

ImmunoPrecise Antibodies [IPA]

ImmunoPrecise Antibodies is the primary working company which serves customers in their pursuit to discover antibody and develop therapies and drug, integrating LensAI and HYFT.

BioStrand

BioStrand is a Belgian subsidiary acquired for approximately €20 million in March 2022, integrates the HYFT technology, including computational tools, knowledge graphs and LensAI.

A more detailed breakdown of LENSai’s potential in epitope mapping and binning, protein structure prediction, immunogenicity screening and antibody development can be found in an presentation presented at the occasion of MindWalk’s 2024 tech day.

Talem

Talem is focused on discovering next-generation monoclonal antibodies for a variety of diseases. Talem’s business model is both in-house and partnering. Talem can use IPA and BioStrand’s technology.

This is Talem’s pipeline, with some drug candidates having been partnered to companies such as Biotheus, Pierre Fabre and OmniAB:

Evidence of execution

In 2025, MindWalk highlighted several LensAI-generated outputs.

In metabolic disease, LensAI produced GLP-1-like constructs optimized for binding, stability, and function within some weeks. Third-party in vitro data reportedly showed semaglutide-class receptor activation, and subsequent analysis suggested a second, longevity-linked pathway that could differentiate the program.

In infectious disease, LensAI identified a single epitope shared across all four dengue serotypes - a potential foundation for a universal vaccine approach - described as structurally stable, immunologically active, and accessible, with preclinical work advancing.

LensAI’s immunogenicity module screens across ~900 HLA variants and the human proteome to flag ADA risk early, with case study performance supportive of prospective utility.

The platform has also predicted binding sites on unseen antibody-antigen complexes without wet lab inputs.

Collectively, these data suggest LensAI can both generate hypotheses and reduce downstream risk.

Financials

At the end of the last quarter, MindWalk reported a record C$7.6 million quarterly revenue, representing 45% growth year-over-year, with 53% margins. This graph shows a comparison with quarters from the years before.

Gross profit was C$4 million, and net loss improved to C$3 million compared to C$4 million the year before. Adjusted EBITDA loss narrowed to C$1.4 million as compared to C$2.8 million the year before.

At the end of the last quarter, the company had C$5 million of cash. Adding C$16.1 million in net proceeds from the divestiture of IPA Netherlands, i.e. $12 million, the company had pro forma C$21 million in cash.

The Yorkville debenture has also been retired.

Given last reported net cash used in operating activities of C$4.2 million and net cash used in investing activities of C$594 million, cash burn for the past quarter was C$4.8 million. On that basis, one could assume that the current cash should be able to provide runway for the company until at least mid-2026.

Because divested operations contributed to prior revenue, I do not assume immediate sequential revenue growth. LensAI bookings, mix and margin and collaboration structure expenses are what I am looking for in next quarter’s results. Margins should improve as the in silico discovery arm has much higher margins. In that sense, I am curious to see the company set out its path to profitability in the years ahead.

Valuation

MindWalk has gone through a number of changes, given the separate businesses, and its recent divestiture. At this time, the market may still value MindWalk as a legacy services business, while its operating model and margin structure increasingly resemble software-led techbio platforms.

LensAI is presumably the most value-generating element of the company’s business. LensAI was only recently commercially launched. As a new AI-tool with high margins – BioStrand has over 90% gross margins - which should still gain awareness with the wider audience, I expect this to be adopted more broadly in the coming years. is in the early stages of commercial development. I believe investors may want to look at it as both a revenue-generating API and a potential pipeline in a software product. Though the initial launch of LensAI did not have much effect on the stock price, the announcements as of June 2025 related to GLP-1 and dengue did seem to have a meaningful impact on the stock.

In that regard, it is noteworthy to note that BioStrand’s growth of revenue went from C$37,627 for fiscal year 2023 to C$1,040,152 for fiscal year 2025.

Simple EV/Sales and Price/Sales screens favor MindWalk, while LensAI remains early in its commercial cycle. My comparison here is with other techbio companies such as Absci Corporation (ABSI) primarily, and Recursion Pharmaceuticals (RXRX) to a lesser extent. These are more known techbio companies, but I believe that with LensAI, MindWalk is playing in the same league.

Absci, for example, currently has a $583 million valuation. It recorded C$30.6 million in net losses in the last quarter, and had C$117.5 million in cash at the end of that quarter. It does not have an ongoing business, and its AI-generated pipeline [slide 12] is still early-stage, with one of four visible drug candidate in Phase 1 studies and the rest in earlier stages of development.

Recursion has a $2.56 billion market cap, with 6 internal drug candidates four of which are in Phase 1 studies, and with further visible partnering and milestone payments with some big pharma companies. Recursion is, by all means, the most visible techbio company in the space, operating the most powerful supercomputer in the pharmaceutical industry and having partnered with Nvidia (NVDA) to that purpose. Nonetheless, while last-reported cash was at $533.8, its expected runway is only until Q4 2027, as quarterly revenue was only $19.2 million and net loss was $171.9 million.

Roivant Sciences (ROIV), which I have covered here before, is in my eyes actually the most successful techbio company, with many subsidiaries and an interesting and further advanced pipeline and a $11.2 billion valuation.

Given the two discoveries this year so far related to GLP-1 and dengue, I assume that it will not be long until LensAI generates a sufficiently large pipeline to partner or to build on internally. Both the GLP-1 and dengue program have large addressable markets, of $53 billion and $2.2 billion respectively.

From a perspective of valuation metrics such as EV/sales and price/sales, I believe that MindWalk is strongly undervalued compared to the other companies I have mentioned above. Data originate from Seeking Alpha’s valuation page for the respective companies.

I believe that MindWalk is heavily undervalued compared to some more visible techbio players.

Risks

Key risks include competition from alternative AI-enabled discovery approaches (including internal programs), and the uncertainty inherent in translating in silico insights into preclinical and clinical outcomes. MindWalk may prioritize partnerships to fund later stages, which could affect timelines and economics.

The progress of AI should not be underestimated, and there are several visible competitors of the company, and probably several internal programs within big pharmaceutical companies that may compete or come to compete with LensAI in the future. The competitivity of the company in the long term is therefore not guaranteed.

While I believe that the reports on LensAI’s output are primarily interesting to customers because of the quality of output generated, it is not excluded that at a later stage, these programs are not partnered or may be discontinued by the company. The company may report disappointing results at a given stage.

While AI clearly cuts development time, and I believe LensAI demonstrates that, preclinical research still needs to be done and time in the clinic will still be needed to ascertain the efficacy of any given drug.

While liquidity improved post-divestiture, the company may not have sufficient cash to push all of its interesting drug candidates forward, and may need to rely on potential partnering to do so. As a result, the company may announce delays with regards to certain assets.

Conclusion

After its recent rebranding and divestiture of the Netherlands wet lab entity, I believe Mindwalk Holdings has pivoted to a platform company with a commercial software suite (LensAI) designed to de-risk and accelerate discovery ahead of wet lab spend as its primary commercial asset. LensAI’s early outputs are the generation of a dual-action GLP-1 agonist, and the basis for a universal dengue vaccine epitope, which appears to be safe, immunologically active, and structurally stable.

The company has an ongoing revenue-generating business which it aims to substantially grow the coming years. LensAI and its HYFT technology are now being used in MindWalk’s different companies, with ImmunoPrecise and BioStrand those in which that software suite should be developed mostly in the coming years. Talem has its own pipeline of drug candidates and is now also using that technology.

With a cleaner balance sheet after the divestiture of its Netherlands wet lab entity and a high-margin software suite, I believe the current valuation undervalues the company’s platform and partnering potential. The company has many partners, including 19 out of 20 big pharma companies, and I am looking forward to how the future revenue/profitability mix would look like in the coming quarters.

For the above reasons, I believe a Buy rating is in place at this time.
